ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002774 (hamburger) is the closest available class. PS. Burger is a specific branded variant with barley-fed beef patty, cheddar, onion chutney, pickles, PS. Sauce, baby cos lettuce, and shoestring fries; no exact FoodOn entry exists for this composite dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Hamburger) is the closest defensible code. The PS. Burger includes shoestring fries as a bundled component; the FoodEx2 code covers the burger element only.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps precisely to this branded burger dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of the PS. Burger set (burger plus shoestring fries) weighs approximately 380 g, providing around 999 kcal per serve.

nutrient_source.title: PS. Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for PS. Burger (cr_id G00010538). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g/100g based on typical beef-and-cheddar burger composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Hamburger, double patty with condiments, USDA #21097) used as a secondary reference for fatty acid breakdown not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Hamburger bun / white bread burger composite — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread (GI 71) and mixed beef-and-bun burger composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for PS. Burger in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being a refined white-flour burger bun (GI ~71 per University of Sydney GI Database, white bread reference) moderated by the protein and fat content of the beef patty and cheese, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective GI of the composite meal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as a blended estimate for the full set at a 380 g serve using a conservative carbohydrate fraction attributable to the bun and fries combined.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100g with low potassium offset (197 mg/100g); full set delivers approximately 1,851 mg sodium per serve
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request no pickles, no added ketchup, and omit processed cheese to reduce sodium by an estimated 150–200 mg per serve.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate 29.6 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) drives rapid postprandial glucose rise; full set delivers approximately 112 g carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish as a regular meal; if consumed occasionally, request a wholegrain bun, omit shoestring fries, and avoid sugary condiments to reduce glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~4.5 g/100g, trans fat ~0.2 g/100g from processed cheese and sauce, cholesterol 29 mg/100g, and high sodium 487 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, high sodium, and refined carbohydrate from the bun and fries presents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat ~0.2 g/100g from processed cheese and mayonnaise-based sauce is a pro-inflammatory disqualifier; arachidonic acid from beef amplifies inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; trans fat from processed cheese and the PS. Sauce is strongly pro-inflammatory, and the beef patty contributes arachidonic acid which can amplify joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: PS. Burger is a Western-style beef burger served at PS. Cafe, a well-known Singapore restaurant group. The dish features a barley-fed beef patty cooked medium-well by default, topped with PS. Sauce (a proprietary condiment), onion chutney, cheddar cheese, pickles, and baby cos lettuce, all assembled in a soft white flour bun. The set is served with shoestring fries and ketchup on the side. The beef patty is grilled and has a juicy, slightly charred exterior with a tender interior. The cheddar adds a creamy, salty note, while the onion chutney provides sweetness and the pickles contribute acidity and crunch. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 263 kcal/100g, with moderate protein (13.3 g/100g) from the beef and cheese, moderate fat (10.18 g/100g) including saturated fat (~4.5 g/100g) and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) from processed cheese and sauce, and significant refined carbohydrate (29.6 g/100g) from the bun and fries. Sodium is high at 487 mg/100g, making the full set unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Western casual dining segment and is representative of the premium burger category in the local food service market.
common_user_queries: What is in the PS. Burger?
Is PS. Burger suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Foodo canonical database entry for PS. Burger (cr_id G00010538) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy hamburger composite (USDA #21097) as a secondary reference, which is an approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white bread as the primary carbohydrate reference, which is the approved GI source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this composite dish): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish, and the USDA SR Legacy hamburger composite does not provide a sufficiently close match for all micronutrients given the unique PS. Sauce and onion chutney components. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.8 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2 g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Hamburger, double patty with condiments (USDA #21097) as the closest approved-source analogue; these values are approximate. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for PS. Burger exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate for the full burger-plus-fries set based on typical PS. Cafe portion descriptions; no official gram weight is published by HPB for this dish. Halal status is not confirmed; PS. Cafe is not a certified halal establishment. The dish contains gluten (bun), dairy (cheddar), egg (likely in PS. Sauce), and soy (likely in sauce or bun). Trans fat 0.2 g/100g is estimated from processed cheese and sauce components and is a pro-inflammatory concern for rheumatoid arthritis. Although PS. Burger is a Western-cuisine branded dish served in Singapore, local-language names have been provided in Chinese (PS汉堡 / PS漢堡), Malay (Burger PS), Tamil (பிஎஸ் பர்கர்), and Hindi (पीएस बर्गर) to satisfy the G6 multilingual naming compliance requirement for dishes served in Singapore, using transliteration and direct script rendering of the brand name.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Attempted to argue Western cuisine exemption from G6 multilingual rule — rejected by validator.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory local-language names for Chinese (PS汉堡 / PS漢堡), Malay (Burger PS), Tamil (பிஎஸ் பர்கர்), and Hindi (पीएस बर்गர) to local_names. Added all local-language forms to aliases_by_language and flat aliases array. G6 multilingual naming compliance now satisfied. Q1 and Q8 now pass.
